title: Behind the Scenes:  Block 450 JVM Repositories into Monorepo to Reduce Dependency Drift
gist: Block consolidated ~450 JVM repositories across Cash App and Square into a single monorepo to kill dependency drift; it now runs ~8,800 builds/week with ~10-min p90 CI on a green main, enabled by a custom IntelliJ plugin, merge queues, and dependency-graph build scoping — and AI agents perform well thanks to the standardized context.

insights.claim: Block consolidated approximately 450 JVM-based repositories across its Cash App and Square engineering organizations into a single monorepo to simplify cross-service development, improve dependency visibility, and eliminate version drift, duplicated upgrade efforts, and diamond-dependency runtime incompatibilities.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: A concrete large-scale polyrepo-to-monorepo migration at a major fintech; a reference data point for the recurring monorepo-vs-polyrepo platform debate.
insights.quote: consolidated approximately 450 JVM-based repositories into a single codebase
insights.locator: Block, Inc. has described a large-scale migration

insights.claim: The Block monorepo now supports approximately 8,800 builds per week with p90 CI times of around 10 minutes on a reliably green main branch, enabling atomic cross-service updates in a single commit and resolving shared dependencies from source rather than independently versioned internal libraries.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Quantifies that a monorepo can stay fast and stable at scale, countering the common assumption that monorepos inevitably slow CI.
insights.quote: the monorepo now supports approximately 8,800 builds per week, with p90 CI times of around 10 minutes on a reliably green main branch
insights.locator: According to the Block engineering team

insights.claim: Block built a custom IntelliJ plugin that loads only the projects an engineer specifies, dynamically swapping project references with published JAR references for dependent projects to keep the IDE fast and avoid slow Gradle configuration (with open-source equivalents at github.com/joshfriend/spotlight and github.com/block/artifact-swap).
insights.speaker: Yissachar Radcliffe
insights.so_what: Project-scoped IDE loading via JAR swapping is the key DevEx trick making large monorepos usable — a reusable pattern with public tooling.
insights.quote: we dynamically swap project references with published JAR references for dependent projects
insights.locator: The custom IntelliJ plugin is a key part

insights.claim: Block scopes builds by checking which files a PR modified against a project dependency graph and categorizing changes as direct, indirect (upstream dependency), or global (core file changes requiring a full build), dropping CI checks for indirect changes that cannot possibly fail.
insights.speaker: Yissachar Radcliffe
insights.so_what: Dependency-graph-based change categorization is how monorepos keep CI cost bounded — a concrete optimization blueprint.
insights.locator: How do you construct and use dependency graphs

insights.claim: Block enforces ownership and boundaries with Gradle convention plugins defining module types (proto, service, library) that constrain the dependency graph (e.g., a service module cannot depend on another service module) and let the platform roll out improvements across all projects at once.
insights.speaker: Yissachar Radcliffe
insights.so_what: Convention-plugin-enforced module boundaries are how a monorepo preserves architecture and enables fleet-wide changes.
insights.locator: how did you maintain clear ownership and service boundaries

insights.claim: Since moving to the monorepo, the IDE has become less of a focus as engineers adopt AI-assisted tooling, and Block reports AI agents perform well in the monorepo because they have access to so much context, with the bet on standardization providing well-paved paths for agents to follow.
insights.speaker: Yissachar Radcliffe
insights.so_what: Monorepo context and standardization may be an underrated enabler of AI coding-agent effectiveness — a new argument in the architecture debate as agents proliferate.
insights.quote: Agents perform well in our monorepo due to having access to so much context, and our bet on standardization has paid off as we have well-paved paths for agents to follow.
insights.locator: have AI-assisted development tools changed how engineers navigate

insights.claim: Block advises against monorepos for teams that cannot fund a dedicated platform team (monorepos suffer badly under neglect) and for portfolios mixing different languages, frameworks, and styles, where a monorepo may not provide significant benefit — monorepos are best for similarly shaped projects.
insights.speaker: Yissachar Radcliffe
insights.so_what: A clear decision rule (platform funding + project homogeneity) for when the monorepo bet pays off — directly actionable architectural guidance.
insights.locator: under what conditions would you advise teams not to adopt a monorepo
